1: 'Every charge must be established by the testimony of two or three witnesses' cites Deuteronomy 17:6 and 2 Corinthians 19:15 (LXX). Paul applies the Mosaic legal standard to the structure of his visits: the third visit will constitute the third 'witness' in Paul's ongoing testimony to the community. In Jewish legal procedure, the two-or-three-witness rule was the foundational standard for any capital case or disciplinary action. By framing his third visit in these terms, Paul signals that this visit will function as a legal proceeding with binding consequences.
2: The warning given on the second (painful) visit is now repeated by letter — 'while absent' (apon) — in lieu of Paul's present voice. The combination of written warning now and promised direct action when present ('I will not spare') replicates the legal pattern of formal warning followed by action. 'Those who sinned earlier and all the others' (pas tous loipous): the discipline will be comprehensive, not limited to the original offenders. The parousia-announcement of Paul's coming functions as an eschatological warning within the letter's rhetorical structure.
3: 'Since you are demanding proof that Christ speaks through me' (dokimen zetountes tou en emoi lalountos Christou): the proof-demand is the climax of the rivalry dispute. The rivals apparently cast doubt on Paul's apostolic credentials by challenging the authenticity of Christ's voice in his ministry. Paul's response: 'he is not weak in dealing with you but is powerful among you.' The power they seek (signs, rhetorical displays) is already present among them in Christ's community-forming work — Paul merely mediated it.
4: The Christ-was-crucified-in-weakness paradox: the epistemological basis for Paul's ministry of weakness. Christ's crucifixion was a demonstration of weakness by every Roman social standard — the condemned criminal hanging in public shame (Cicero Pro Rabirio 5.16 calls crucifixion the most extreme penalty reserved for non-citizens). Yet he 'lives by the power of God' (ze ek dynameoas theou). Paul's apostolic existence follows the same pattern: weak in Christ, yet powerful by the same resurrection-power when dealing with the community's need. Weakness and power coexist in the apostolic and Christological mode.
5: The self-examination instruction: 'examine yourselves' (heautous peirazetie). Peirazete = to test, try, put to the proof — the same root as the 'testing' of Jesus in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1) and of faith in James 1:2. The community is invited to apply to themselves the test they wanted to apply to Paul. 'Do you not realize that Jesus Christ is in you?' (en hymin) — the apostolic test reverses: the community's own inner life is the evidence for Christ's presence and therefore for the apostle's authenticity. If Christ is in them, he got there through Paul's ministry.
6: 'I hope you will discover that we have not failed the test' (hoti hemeis ouk esmen adokimoi): adokimos = failing the test, counterfeit (as of base metal failing the assay). The irony: Paul hopes the Corinthians' self-examination will simultaneously vindicate Paul's apostolicity — because the Christ in them was implanted through Paul's gospel. The community's spiritual life is Paul's credentialing evidence; the super-apostles who have not founded the community have no comparable evidence.
7: Paul's stated preference is not to demonstrate apostolic power through discipline but for the community to do what is right on its own. The prayer is for their moral integrity, not for the opportunity to exercise authority. 'Even if we appear to have failed the test' (hemeis de hos adokimoi omen) — even if Paul appears powerless (because no discipline was needed), that is acceptable if the community is living rightly. The apostle's power is always exercised for building up, not for demonstration (oikodomein eis oikodomen).
8: 'We cannot work against the truth, only for it' (ou gar dynametha ti kata tes aletheias, alla hyper tes aletheias): a gnome (general maxim) that grounds all apostolic practice in the truth's own direction. The apostle cannot exercise authority contrary to the truth — not because of personal limitation but because the truth itself constrains and orients all genuine apostolic work. If the community is walking rightly, Paul can only rejoice, not find occasions to punish.
9: 'We are glad whenever we are weak and you are strong' — the reversal of competitive rhetoric: Paul's gladness in his own weakness is genuine when it corresponds to the community's strength. The prayer for 'your complete restoration' (hymon katartisin): katartisis (from katartizo = to mend, restore, put in order; used for mending nets in Matthew 4:21) — the community as a net that needs repairing, or as a dislocated joint being set back in place. Complete restoration is the pastoral goal, not punishment or apostolic vindication.
10: The letter's stated purpose: written while absent so Paul need not be harsh when present. The authority (exousia) given by the Lord is for building up (oikodome), not tearing down (kathairesis) — a principle stated in 10:8 and restated here to close the letter's argument. The authority to build up is the governing constraint on all apostolic power: if Paul must use authority destructively (exercising judgment), something has already gone wrong in the community. The preferred outcome is always the upbuilding that makes discipline unnecessary.
11: The closing exhortations cluster Greek imperatives: 'farewell/rejoice' (chairete), 'aim for restoration' (katartizesthe — the same root as 2 Corinthians 13:9's katartisis), 'encourage one another' (parakaliesthe), 'be of one mind' (to auto phroneite), 'live in peace' (eireneuete). The final four are all present imperatives — ongoing community practices, not one-time acts. The promise: 'the God of love and peace will be with you' (ho theos tes agapes kai eirenes estai meth' hymon) — the character of God (agape and eirene) corresponds to the community's called practice.
12: 'Greet one another with a holy kiss' (en hagio philemati): the holy kiss appears in all four of Paul's closing greetings (Romans 16:16; 1 Corinthians 16:20; 1 Thessalonians 5:26; here) and in 1 Peter 5:14. The kiss was a standard Mediterranean greeting gesture for family members and close associates — Paul applies it to the community of believers as a sign of fictive kinship in Christ. It also functioned liturgically: Justin Martyr (First Apology 65) describes the kiss of peace as part of the eucharistic assembly. The practice bridges private greeting and public worship.
13: 'All God's people here send their greetings' — the community with Paul (probably in Macedonia) co-signs the greeting. This counters the implicit claim of the rivals that they represent a larger apostolic network: Paul writes not alone but with a community behind him. The plural senders also ground the letter in a specific church context, not just in an individual apostle's authority.
14: The closing Trinitarian benediction: 'the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit' (he charis tou Kyriou Iesou Christou kai he agape tou theou kai he koinonia tou hagiou pneumatos). This is the most explicit three-person blessing formula in Paul's letters and one of the earliest Trinitarian formulas in Christian literature. The three gifts are matched to the three persons: charis (grace) to Christ, agape (love) to God, koinonia (fellowship, participation) to the Spirit. The order — Christ, God, Spirit — is not subordinationist but reflects the order of Christian experience: encounter with Christ (through the gospel) leads to the knowledge of God's love and to participation in the Spirit's life.
